objective: clear lead vocal with safe feedback margin
steps: Set preamp so the loudest chorus peaks cleanly before the clip light threatens.
Trim rumble first, then pull muddy low mids before adding any vocal presence.
Use a short hall reverb sparingly so the singer feels support without washing out diction.
Solve feedback with mic position and speaker placement before chasing more gain.
Leave a little headroom for louder songs or crowd-noise spikes.
notes: Reliable starting scene for compact rooms where clarity matters more than size.

objective: support inconsistent singers without losing intelligibility
steps: Keep the preamp conservative so sudden loud singers still have headroom.
Start flat, cut mud before boosting highs, and avoid over-shaping for any one voice.
Use a short vocal reverb or delay for confidence, but keep the tail controlled.
Keep speakers well in front of the microphone and watch for cupped-mic feedback.
Favor consistency over perfection because the singer changes every song.
notes: This preset is for rotation and survivability, not for dialing in one perfect vocalist.

objective: keep clue calls and transitions intelligible over backing tracks

context.source: host vocal mic and playback laptop

steps: Set speech gain for strong announcements with clean peaks and enough space for excited moments.
Cut mud first and add only enough presence to keep clue calls crisp over the track.
Keep host effects nearly dry so every instruction stays easy to parse.
Ride the playback level under the host instead of asking the mic channel to overpower the room.
Use speaker placement and conservative monitor level to stay ahead of feedback.
notes: A host-driven event wins on diction, pacing, and transitions more than sonic size.

objective: balanced intimacy with low stage volume

context.sources: lead vocal and DI acoustic guitar

steps: Build the vocal first, then add guitar until it supports the lyric instead of competing with it.
High-pass both channels enough to clear rumble while keeping warmth in the guitar body.
Use gentle ambience only; the room should still feel close and conversational.
Keep stage volume low so the front-of-house mix stays stable from song to song.
Listen for harsh pick attack or nasal vocal mids before boosting any sparkle.
notes: Useful when intimacy and lyric focus matter more than impact.
